refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall update the worksite model to reflect current equipment state, environmental conditions, and operational events with a p95 latency not exceeding the customer-defined target (baseline 1.0 s) across all supported update scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-007 addresses a critical operational requirement: timely situational awareness for safe and effective equipment operation. The refined statement explicitly bounds the latency constraint (p95 ≤ 1.0 s), identifies the scope of updates (equipment state, environment, events), and acknowledges customer-specific tailoring. High safety criticality demands that latency failures be detectable and that the system gracefully degrade rather than silently stale.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency from sensor acquisition through worksite model update across representative scenarios (e.g., position update, obstacle detection, operator command acknowledgment). Validate p95 percentile under nominal and degraded network conditions. Confirm that stale data is flagged to operators.
sub_frs: FR-007.1: Acquire and transmit telemetry from vehicle to worksite model with bounded latency.
FR-007.2: Process and integrate telemetry into worksite model with bounded latency.
FR-007.3: Detect and alert on latency violations or data staleness.
FR-007.4: Ensure network link availability and quality to support latency target.

candidate_dps: DP-007.A: Implement real-time telemetry pipeline with prioritized message queuing, local buffering on vehicle, and edge processing to minimize end-to-end latency.
DP-007.B: Deploy distributed worksite model with eventual consistency and conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs) to tolerate network jitter.
DP-007.C: Establish dedicated low-latency network link (e.g., 5G, private LTE) with QoS guarantees and fallback to secondary link on primary failure.
DP-007.D: Implement latency monitoring and operator alerting via SafetyAlert interface when p95 threshold is exceeded or data staleness detected.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-007: Integrate real-time telemetry pipeline (DP-007.A) with distributed worksite model (DP-007.B), dedicated network link with QoS (DP-007.C), and latency monitoring with safety alerting (DP-007.D).

potential_sources: Telemetry acquisition rate and message format must align with worksite model schema to avoid transformation overhead.
Network link QoS parameters (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) must be dimensioned to support telemetry throughput and latency target.
Latency monitoring thresholds must be calibrated to customer operational requirements and safety policies.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP is a composition of four sub-DPs addressing acquisition, processing, transport, and monitoring. Coupling is managed through well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert) and explicit QoS contracts. No circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer will define acceptable latency target (baseline 1.0 s p95) and provide representative update scenarios for validation.
ASSUMPTION: Network infrastructure (5G/LTE) is available at worksite or can be provisioned by customer.
RISK: If network link is congested or unreliable, latency target may not be achievable without reducing telemetry frequency or resolution, impacting situational awareness.
RISK: Distributed worksite model with eventual consistency may introduce transient inconsistencies visible to operators, requiring clear UI/UX design to avoid confusion.
RISK: Latency monitoring overhead (sampling, aggregation, alerting) may itself contribute to latency if not carefully implemented.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-007.B to specify consistency model (e.g., causal consistency for safety-critical updates, eventual consistency for non-critical state). Refine DP-007.C to include fallback strategy (e.g., degrade to lower-frequency updates or local-only mode if primary link fails). Refine DP-007.D to specify alert thresholds and operator response procedures.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Teleoperation module owns the real-time telemetry pipeline, worksite model synchronization, network link management, and latency monitoring. It integrates vehicle telemetry (via Telemetry interface), manages network transport (via Network Link interface), and triggers safety alerts (via SafetyAlert interface). The module is responsible for end-to-end latency from vehicle sensor to worksite model update.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle telemetry (position, orientation, sensor readings, operational state) flows from Vehicle module to Teleoperation module at high frequency (e.g., 10–100 Hz). Teleoperation module buffers and prioritizes telemetry for worksite model update.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link to transmit telemetry and receive worksite model updates. Network Link provides QoS parameters (latency, jitter, packet loss) and fallback mechanisms.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module emits SafetyAlert when latency exceeds threshold or data staleness is detected. Safety Services module consumes alerts and notifies operators and Safety Manager.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (via Tasking interface) and prioritizes them relative to telemetry updates to ensure responsive control.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Latency management is a cross-cutting concern that spans Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and Network Link. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and obscure ownership. Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle module owns telemetry acquisition and local buffering. However, worksite model synchronization, network link management, and latency monitoring are outside Vehicle scope. Vehicle is a producer of telemetry, not the owner of end-to-end latency. Partial containment only.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of real-time telemetry pipeline, worksite model synchronization, and latency monitoring. It sits at the intersection of Vehicle (telemetry source), Network Link (transport), and Safety Services (alerting). High containment: all sub-DPs (acquisition pipeline, distributed model, network QoS, monitoring) are within Teleoperation scope. Low diffusion: minimal spillover to other modules.

Safety Services owns safety alerting and policy enforcement. Latency monitoring and alerting (DP-007.D) is a safety function, but the underlying telemetry pipeline and network management are not Safety Services concerns. Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert from Teleoperation; it does not own the latency mechanism. Not recommended as primary owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of CR-NGHE-007 (worksite update latency). It owns the telemetry pipeline (acquisition, buffering, prioritization), worksite model synchronization, network link management, and latency monitoring. All four sub-DPs (DP-007.A through DP-007.D) are naturally contained within Teleoperation's scope. External interfaces (Telemetry in, Network Link inout, SafetyAlert out, Tasking in) are well-defined and minimize coupling to other modules.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if latency monitoring or worksite model synchronization is not efficiently implemented. Recommend profiling and optimization during detailed design.

RISK: Network Link interface must provide sufficient QoS visibility (latency, jitter, packet loss) for Teleoperation to make intelligent buffering and prioritization decisions. If Network Link is opaque, Teleoperation cannot adapt. Recommend explicit QoS contract in Network Link interface specification.

RISK: Vehicle module must provide telemetry at sufficient frequency and with accurate timestamps for Teleoperation to measure end-to-end latency. If Vehicle telemetry is sparse or timestamps are unreliable, latency measurement will be inaccurate. Recommend Telemetry interface specification to include timestamp precision and frequency requirements.

RISK: Safety Services must respond promptly to SafetyAlert from Teleoperation. If Safety Services is slow to process alerts, the latency benefit of Teleoperation's monitoring is lost. Recommend SafetyAlert interface to specify alert processing SLA.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine Teleoperation module boundary to explicitly include: (1) telemetry ingestion and buffering, (2) worksite model state machine and synchronization logic, (3) network link QoS monitoring and adaptation, (4) latency measurement and alerting. Specify external interfaces with QoS contracts: Telemetry (frequency, timestamp precision), Network Link (latency, jitter, packet loss visibility), SafetyAlert (alert latency SLA), Tasking (command priority). Document assumptions about Vehicle telemetry quality and Network Link availability.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (real-time telemetry pipeline + distributed worksite model + dedicated network link + latency monitoring) directly addresses the FR (worksite model update latency ≤ 1.0 s p95). The DP is decomposed into four sub-DPs that collectively cover acquisition, processing, transport, and monitoring. No functional gaps detected. Coupling is managed through well-defined interfaces and QoS contracts.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module owns the telemetry pipeline and worksite model synchronization, but Vehicle module owns telemetry acquisition. Interface contract (Telemetry) must be clear to avoid latency creep due to misalignment. Recommend explicit SLA on Telemetry interface (frequency, timestamp precision, buffering behavior).

Network Link is a separate module, but Teleoperation depends on its QoS visibility. If Network Link does not expose latency/jitter/packet loss metrics, Teleoperation cannot adapt. Recommend explicit QoS metrics in Network Link interface.

Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert from Teleoperation but does not own latency monitoring. Recommend clear ownership boundary: Teleoperation measures and alerts, Safety Services responds and enforces policy.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.85) for CR-NGHE-007. The four sub-DPs (telemetry pipeline, worksite model, network QoS, monitoring) are all within Teleoperation scope. External dependencies (Vehicle telemetry, Network Link, Safety Services alerting) are minimal and well-defined via interfaces. Diffusion is low (0.15), indicating good architectural packaging.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-007 is feasible within Teleoperation module scope. Latency can be measured end-to-end from Vehicle telemetry ingestion to worksite model update. Acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ 1.0 s) can be validated via instrumentation and testing under representative scenarios. Stale data detection can be verified via unit and integration tests.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of CR-NGHE-007 to Teleoperation module is clear. Teleoperation owns the telemetry pipeline, worksite model synchronization, and latency monitoring. Vehicle module provides telemetry input (via Telemetry interface). Network Link module provides transport (via Network Link interface). Safety Services module consumes alerts (via SafetyAlert interface). Ownership boundaries are explicit and non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Refine Telemetry interface specification to include: telemetry frequency (e.g., 10–100 Hz), timestamp precision (e.g., ±10 ms), and buffering behavior (e.g., FIFO, priority queue). This ensures Vehicle module provides telemetry suitable for latency measurement.

Refine Network Link interface specification to expose QoS metrics: latency (p50, p95, p99), jitter, packet loss, and link state (up/down/degraded). This enables Teleoperation to adapt buffering and prioritization strategies.

Refine SafetyAlert interface specification to include alert latency SLA (e.g., Safety Services must process alert within 100 ms). This ensures end-to-end latency benefit is not lost in alert handling.

Document assumptions about customer-defined latency target (baseline 1.0 s p95) and representative update scenarios. Recommend customer engagement to confirm target and scenarios before detailed design.
Recommend profiling and optimization plan for Teleoperation module to ensure latency target is achievable under nominal and degraded network conditions.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-007 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (telemetry acquisition, model processing, latency detection, network availability) that must be individually designed and verified. The selected DP is a composition of four sub-DPs that address these sub-FRs. Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner with high containment (0.85) and low diffusion (0.15). All gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass. Proceed to next level of decomposition for each sub-FR.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-007.1: Acquire and transmit telemetry from vehicle to worksite model with bounded latency (candidate DP: high-frequency telemetry pipeline with local buffering and prioritization on Vehicle and Teleoperation modules).

FR-007.2: Process and integrate telemetry into worksite model with bounded latency (candidate DP: distributed worksite model with causal consistency for safety-critical updates and eventual consistency for non-critical state).
FR-007.3: Detect and alert on latency violations or data staleness (candidate DP: latency monitoring with threshold-based alerting via SafetyAlert interface).

FR-007.4: Ensure network link availability and quality to support latency target (candidate DP: dedicated low-latency network link with QoS guarantees and fallback to secondary link on primary failure).

assumptions: Customer will define acceptable latency target (baseline 1.0 s p95) and provide representative update scenarios for validation.
Network infrastructure (5G/LTE) is available at worksite or can be provisioned by customer.
Vehicle module provides telemetry at sufficient frequency (10–100 Hz) with accurate timestamps (±10 ms precision).
Network Link module exposes QoS metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss) for Teleoperation to adapt buffering and prioritization.
Safety Services module processes SafetyAlert within acceptable latency (e.g., 100 ms) to avoid cascading delays.
Worksite model schema is stable and supports incremental updates without full re-synchronization.
open_risks: Network link congestion or unreliability may prevent latency target from being achieved without reducing telemetry frequency or resolution.
Distributed worksite model with eventual consistency may introduce transient inconsistencies visible to operators, requiring careful UI/UX design.
Latency monitoring overhead may itself contribute to latency if not carefully implemented (e.g., avoid synchronous logging or blocking measurements).
Vehicle telemetry quality (frequency, timestamp precision) may be insufficient for accurate latency measurement.
Safety Services may not respond promptly to SafetyAlert, negating the latency benefit of Teleoperation's monitoring.
evidence_needed: Customer confirmation of latency target (p95 ≤ 1.0 s) and representative update scenarios.
Network infrastructure assessment: availability, QoS capabilities, fallback options.
Vehicle telemetry specification: frequency, timestamp precision, buffering behavior.
Worksite model schema and synchronization protocol specification.
Safety Services alert processing SLA and operator response procedures.
Latency measurement methodology and instrumentation plan.
Performance profiling and optimization plan for Teleoperation module.
